fr:title.text: Operator fusion is like ASIC caching

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Operator fusion is like ASIC caching

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On a traditional CPU, the hardware manages caching to ensure that data that is reused does not roundtrip through DRAM. On an ASIC accelerators, we forego the complexity of caching to allow for more execution units. However, now if we have two operations i.e. elementwise add \to softmax this would

html:ol.html:li: Load the input tensors from memory (HBM on TPU).
Store the output tensor to memory.
Load the input tensors from memory.
Store the output tensor to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On a CPU, the input tensors would remain in the cache. However, this must be done explicitly on ASIC (or by the compiler in the case of Google / XLA).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: 🌱 Stub — this concept is referenced but not yet written.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Application-Specific Integrated Circuits fuse multiple operations into hardware that a general-purpose pipeline would have to schedule sequentially. The motivating intuition: if you frequently multiply-accumulate, the multiply and the add each traverse the entire pipeline separately. A dedicated multiply-accumulate ALU does both in one cycle.
The cost-of-operation gap is large — 32-bit vs 8-bit addition has roughly an order of magnitude difference in energy per op. ASICs win by matching width to need.

html:ul.html:li: Vector register file each register is 8x128 vector registers with 8 horizontal lanes and 128 vertical lanes. There are typically 64 of these registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for elementwise operations.

html:ul.html:li: 2D SIMD execution, where each of 1024 sites execute same program in lockstep.
Each site contains 4 ALUs, enabling 4096 FLOPs.
Fed a 8x128 Vector Register in one cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for performing matrix multiplication via a systolic array.

html:ul.html:li: 256x256 totaling 65,536 multiply accumulate units i.e. each unit can multiply and add in one cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. Can only do 256x256 matrix multiplication, so the order is
Tiling the matrix to load from HBM to VMEM
Compiler tiles from VMEM to 256x256 tiles to load into MXU.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Note the input are offset so that the result from the first column can be accumulated int the second column. Can compute matmul in 2n - 1 cycles.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notes on guest lecture by Yufeng Gu titled Architecture and System Co-Design for Scalable Large Language Model Inference
TFLOPs increasing much faster than DRAM bandwidth -> Hardware scaling is hitting the memory wall.
Data center power consumption also increasing \approx 10% a year.

html:p.html:strong: GPUs are under low utilization for LLM inference

html:ul.html:li: Prefix stage: parallel
Decoding stage: sequential -> bottleneck!

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As batch size increases, the KV-cache dominates memory usage.

html:ul.html:li: KV cache size grows with context length.
Tradeoff between context length and batch size.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Unlike fully-connected cannot batch self-attention because it depends on the individual prompts.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Recall. operational intensity is \frac {\text {compute throughput}}{\text {memory bandwidth}}

html:p.html:strong: Inference much lower operational intensity than GEMM!

html:ul.html:li: Inference only doing matrix \times row
If embedding dim is D, then this is only D ops / byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Processing in memory (PIM) enables faster memory bandwidth.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Has lower memory capacity (fewer GB) due to added processing units (PU).
Solution. Use Compute eXpress Link (CXL) to do memory expansion to get high memory bandwidth + expend memory capacity.

Remark. Put high use operations i.e. vector multiplication, element-wise multiplication in PIM module (99% usage). Put more complicated operations on PNM accelerator. TODO: ISAs for each specified on slides (not available yet).
Remark. CENT supports inter and intra-device communication via connecting communication controller to shared buffer and CXL port.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Communcation primitives. multicast and gather

html:ul.html:li: Multicast: send to all CXL devices.
Gather: receive data sequentially from all CXL devices.
Multicsat-gather -> tensor parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Peer-to-peer: can also support pipeline parallelism.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. For fast communication, use tensor parallelism i.e. if connected via NVLink. If slower communication use pipeline parallelism. Can mix.
Remark. Longer context -> better performance for PIM (TODO: why?). HBM + PIM does not scale. Cannot arbitrarily add PIM.

html:ul.html:li: Dispatch / Cache: caches compilation based on input argument signatures i.e. shapes, dtypes, sharding. If shapes change will need to recompile.
XLA transformation: Python -> JaxPR -> StableHLO, performing fusion and other simplifications to reduce memory overhead (?).
TPU specific: Further reduced to LLO IR, then very large instruction word (VLIW) scheduling to map dot products to MXU.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. As soon as python code is traced into jaxpr it is thrown away. Only HLO is input to XLA compiler.

html:ul.html:li: Abstract execution. JAX executes python code using abstract tracer objects instead of real arrays.
Recording the FLow. Every operation and its intermediate result is recorded into JAXpr.
Sequence used to construct a grap, which the XLA compiler will later optimize.
Python code is executed once during tracing but excluded from final graph.
Each JAX primitive registers functions for
Lowering to StableHLO
How it is transformed under grad and vmap.
Multiple ML frameworks lower to StableHLO.
Hardware agnostic.
Backwards compatible.

html:ol.html:li: Compile: HLO -> Optimized HLO -> LLO IR -> VLIW -> Executables

html:ul.html:li: compiled.runtime_executable() returns a LoadedExecutable.
Useful(?) compiled.cost_analysis().
HLO:
Input for XLA compiler.
Directed acyclic graph of copmutational operations.
All array dimensions are known at compile time.
Limited opcodes i.e. Add, Multiply, Transpose, Dot, etc.
Every data type defined by data type and fixed dimensions, determined at compile time.
Minor-to-major order i.e. fastest changing to slowesting changin.
Tiling: arrays are split into smaller blocks to fit into VREG. Want to arrange data to efficiently fit VREG.

html:figure.html:figcaption: How memory is laid out based on XLA.

html:ul.html:li: Move tensors from CPU to HBM. Sharding is taken care of by PJIT if on TPU pod.
Async: Immediately returns a Future JAX array while the hardware executes VLIW microcode asynchronously.
Buffer management: Returns pointer to TPU memory. All handled lazily. Not copied back to CPU until jax.block_until_ready().

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU clock is decreasing much faster than main-memory latency, so without a cache hierarchy a fast CPU spends most of its time stalled on memory.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. If you are a really fast factory, the size of the truck doesn't matter — you must still wait the full duration of the first delivery.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple tiers of progressively-larger, progressively-slower storage between the CPU's registers and main memory. Each tier holds the most-recently-used contents of the next slower tier, exploiting Spatial locality and Temporal locality in the workload.
Approximate tiers on a modern x86 chip:

html:ul.html:li: Registers — ns, dozens of bytes per core.
L1 cache — 2–8 ns, 8–128 KB per core. Often split into instruction and data L1s.
Built from static RAM (4–6 transistors per cell), which is fast but area-expensive vs. DRAM's single-transistor cell.
L2 cache — 5–12 ns, 0.5–8 MB. Closes the latency gap between L1 and DRAM.
L3 cache — slower, shared across cores.
Main memory — 10–60 ns, GBs.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. A byte's journey. L1 checked first; on miss, L2; on miss, main memory; on miss in main memory, Page fault to disk.

Remark. L1 is split into instruction and data caches because the locality profiles of code and data are very different — re-running the same code over different data has high temporal locality for code but low for data.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A cache miss happens when the requested data isn't in cache and has to be fetched from a slower tier. Misses fall into four categories — the "four Cs" of cache misses:

html:ol.html:li: Compulsory miss — the data was never in cache. Cold-start misses are unavoidable for a given cache size and working set.
Conflict miss — the data was evicted because of an associativity collision. A different access mapped to the same set kicked it out. Reduced by higher Cache associativity.
Coherence miss — the data was invalidated because another core wrote to it. Specific to shared-memory multicore systems.
Capacity miss — the cache filled up and the data was evicted by Cache replacement policy. The working set doesn't fit.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Distinguishing them matters because they have different mitigations: conflict misses respond to associativity changes, capacity misses respond to size, coherence misses respond to cache-coherence protocol design, and compulsory misses respond to prefetching.

The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
If a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access x again soon. Loop variables, frequently-called functions, and re-used data structures all exhibit it.

Caches exploit temporal locality by retaining recently-accessed lines until something more important takes their slot. The Cache replacement policy is what decides "more important" — typically LRU and the clock algorithm-ish.
Temporal locality implies spatial locality (re-touching a byte tends to re-touch its neighbors) but not vice versa: a sequential scan has spatial locality with no temporal locality.
The rule that decides which slots in the cache an address is allowed to occupy. Three points on the spectrum:

html:ul.html:li: Direct mapped — every address maps to exactly one slot. Fast (only one tag to check) but suffers conflict misses: two hot addresses that map to the same slot evict each other.
Fully associative — any address can live in any slot. No conflict misses but tag check is expensive (compare against every entry in parallel).

n-way set associative — middle ground. The cache is divided into sets of n slots; an address maps to a specific set but can occupy any of its n slots. The hot setting on real caches is somewhere between 4-way and 16-way.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Higher associativity reduces conflict misses but increases tag-check hardware and power. The cache tag of an address is the bits that aren't used to pick the set — they're stored alongside the data and compared on every access.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The tag array is stored separately from the data array (tag RAM) so the tag comparison can happen in parallel with the data lookup. Tags are small, allowing many in-flight comparisons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Within a Cache associativity set, when a new line arrives and the set is full, which existing line gets evicted? Same question as LRU and the clock algorithm for pages, different timescales.

The hot policy is least recently used — but pure LRU requires tracking a per-line ordering and updating it on every hit, which is too expensive at L1 latencies. Real caches use pseudo-LRU — a tree of single bits per set that approximates LRU with a fraction of the bookkeeping. The clock algorithm is one well-known pseudo-LRU scheme.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Block (cache line) size is part of the same tradeoff. Larger blocks improve Spatial locality but cause cache pollution (you fetch bytes you don't use) and eat memory bandwidth. The sweet spot is around 64 bytes on modern x86.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When a cached line is modified, when do you propagate the change to the next tier?

html:ul.html:li: Write-through — every store updates both the cache and main memory. Simple, but generates memory traffic on every write.

Write-back — stores update only the cache. The line is marked dirty via a per-line bit; the next-tier copy is updated only when the line is evicted. Less memory traffic but the cache is now the sole authoritative source until eviction, which complicates correctness.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Real CPUs run write-back at L1 and L2. Write-through is occasionally used for specific lines (e.g. memory-mapped device registers where every write must hit the device immediately).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Write-back is one of the constraints behind cache-coherence protocols on multicore chips: every core must agree on which one currently owns a dirty line.

html:ul.html:li: Vector register file each register is 8x128 vector registers with 8 horizontal lanes and 128 vertical lanes. There are typically 64 of these registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for elementwise operations.

html:ul.html:li: 2D SIMD execution, where each of 1024 sites execute same program in lockstep.
Each site contains 4 ALUs, enabling 4096 FLOPs.
Fed a 8x128 Vector Register in one cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A core component of the TPU architecture responsible for performing matrix multiplication via a systolic array.

html:ul.html:li: 256x256 totaling 65,536 multiply accumulate units i.e. each unit can multiply and add in one cycle.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. Can only do 256x256 matrix multiplication, so the order is
Tiling the matrix to load from HBM to VMEM
Compiler tiles from VMEM to 256x256 tiles to load into MXU.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Note the input are offset so that the result from the first column can be accumulated int the second column. Can compute matmul in 2n - 1 cycles.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The softmax function \mathrm {softmax}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is defined by \mathrm {softmax}(\mathbf {x})_i = \frac {e^{x_i}}{\sum _{j = 1}^{d} e^{x_j}}
